---
title: "Week 0 Background Report"
format: html
editor: visual
---

# A. Introduction

My name is Shubho Banerjee and I am a sophomore at Oak Park High School. I am interested in Mathematics and English, and my goal for this internship is to learn how to connect these two fields to each other and apply them to health data science.

# B. Reproducible Research

#### Q: What does Reproducible Research Mean to Me?

A: To me, reproducible research means having full transparency into the exact materials and methodologies other scientists used to achieve their results. This openness allows us to verify past findings, replicate experiments accurately, produce consistent results, and build on other scientists' work. This practice is essential for society because it improves reliability and public trust in research.

# C. Small R Exercise

```{r}
# Load the ggplot2 package for data visualization
library(ggplot2)
# Create a scatter plot using the built-in mtcars dataset
ggplot(data = mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point(color = "royalblue", size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Car Weight vs. Fuel Efficiency",
    x = "Weight (1,000 lbs)",
    y = "Fuel Efficiency (Miles per Gallon)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The scatter plot shows a clear downward trend. Lighter vehicles are higher on the Y-axis, indicating that they have higher fuel efficiency. Heavier vehicles are lower on the Y-axis, indicating that they have lower fuel efficiency.

# D. Background Memo

```{css, echo=FALSE}
.page-break {
  page-break-before: always;
  break-before: page;
}
.memo-container {
  max-width: 800px;
  margin: 0 auto;
  line-height: 1.6;
}
```

:::: {.page-break .memo-container}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {style="text-align: center; font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.5em; letter-spacing: 2px;"}
MEMORANDUM
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**TO:** Dr. Jin and Hua Zhou\
**FROM:** Shubho Banerjee, High School Intern\
**DATE:** June 30, 2026\
**SUBJECT:** Clinical Utility of Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) vs. HbA1c

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Introduction

The purpose of this memo is to explain what continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) can measure that a traditional Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) test cannot. While the HbA1c test remains a standard measure for long-term blood sugar control, it only provides an average covering a three-month period. Because of this, the real-time glucose fluctuations that occur in a person's body daily can be hidden.

### Dynamic Metrics: Beyond the Average

The primary limitation of HbA1c is its inability to capture glucose variability. For example, two individuals can have an identical, healthy HbA1c average of 6.5%. However, one person may have stable blood sugar all day, while the other may have more volatile blood sugar, fluctuating between extreme highs and lows.

CGM captures these real-time variations by tracking Time in Range (TIR), which is the exact percentage of the time a person's glucose stays within a safe target window (usually 70–180 mg/dL). Crucially, CGMs can detect hypoglycemia (dangerously low blood sugar). Because an HbA1c test only shows an average glucose level, severe overnight drops in blood sugar go undetected.

### Real-Time Behavioral Responses and Patterns

Unlike a single lab test, CGMs record data every few minutes, revealing daily glucose patterns. This allows patients and providers to see exactly how the body responds to immediate factors in our lifestyles:

- **Food and Post-Meal Spikes:** CGMs show rapid glucose spikes after eating certain carbohydrates, helping individuals identify which foods trigger larger glucose spikes.
- **Sleep and Stress:** High-stress situations or poor sleep release cortisol, causing temporary increases in glucose levels on a CGM graph.
- **Exercise and Medication:** Users can watch how physical activity lowers blood sugar in real time, helping them better manage the timing of medications or insulin doses to avoid crashes.

### Conclusion

In the end, HbA1c is a helpful diagnostic marker, but it only looks backward at a broad average. CGM provides continuous real-time glucose data rather than a single average measurement. By revealing short-term responses to food, stress, and exercise, CGM can provide patients with information they can use to make immediate decisions to improve their health.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
::::

# E. Paper Summary

```{css, echo=FALSE}
.page-break {
  page-break-before: always;
  break-before: page;
}
.memo-container {
  max-width: 800px;
  margin: 0 auto;
  line-height: 1.6;
}
```

:::: {.page-break .memo-container}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {style="text-align: center; font-weight: bold; font-size: 1.5em; letter-spacing: 1px;"}
SCIENTIFIC PAPER SUMMARY
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Paper Title:** *Change in Hemoglobin A1c and Quality of Life with Real-Time Continuous Glucose Monitoring Use by People with Insulin-Treated Diabetes in the LANDMARK Study*\
**Authors:** Behavioral Diabetes Institute Research Group\
**Journal:** *Diabetes Technology & Therapeutics* / American Diabetes Association Center

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1. Main Research Question

The primary goal of the LANDMARK study was to evaluate the real-world clinical impact of initiating a real-time Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) system in adults with Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes. Specifically, the researchers aimed to measure changes in blood sugar control (via HbA1c levels) alongside changes in the patients' psychological well-being and diabetes-related emotional distress.

### 2. Data Used

The study analyzed a diverse group of real-world patients managing their diabetes with intensive insulin therapy:

- **Participants:** Adults diagnosed with either Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes who had HbA1C levels above 7.0%.

- **Device:** Participants used the Dexcom G6 real-time CGM system.

- **Timeline:** Patient data, lab work, and psychological surveys were collected at baseline and monitored over a 12-week (3-month) study period.

### 3. Methods

This was a multicenter clinical study tracking real-world outcomes. At the start of the study (week zero), participants had their baseline HbA1c measured and completed standardized psychological questionnaires, including the Diabetes Distress Scale (DDS) and the Hypoglycemia Fear Survey (HABS). To reflect a real world environment, participants received no standardized training or structured clinical interventions when they started using the CGM. Over the 3-month period, the CGM automatically recorded continuous data. At week 12, final blood draws and psychological surveys were administered, and paired t-tests were used to evaluate changes from baseline.

### 4. Key Findings

The study found significant improvements in both glycemic control and diabetes-related emotional well-being at the end of the 12 weeks:

- **Significant HbA1c Reduction:** The mean absolute HbA1c level decreased from 8.2% at baseline down to 7.1% at the conclusion of the study.

- **High Success Rate:** More than half (54.4%) of the participants who started with elevated HbA1c levels achieved an absolute HbA1c drop greater than 1.0%.

- **Reduced Mental Burden:** Patients reported a statistically significant reduction in both general diabetes distress (DDS) and specific fears regarding unexpected episodes of hypoglycemia.

- **User Experience:** 93% of participants reported being highly satisfied with the technology, and 73% found it very easy to integrate into their routines without formal training.

### 5. Limitations

The authors identified two key limitations to consider when interpreting the results:

1\. **Short Evaluation Window:** The study only tracked patients for 3 months. This timeline was long enough to capture an HbA1c cycle change, but it is too short to determine if these improvements will be maintained over the long term.

2\. **Lack of a Control Group:** Because this was a prospective observational tracking study rather than a blinded, randomized controlled trial (RCT), it is difficult to completely rule out other factors that might have influenced a patient's lifestyle choices during those 12 weeks.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
::::

# F. AI-READI Reflection

#### Q: What can AI-READI help researchers understand, and what can it not prove?

A: AI-READI helps researchers understand relationships between daily lifestyles and diabetes health. Its diverse data also helps improve the generalizability of new medical machine learning models. However, this observational data cannot establish causation. Unmeasured variables and sensor measurement limits mean the data never tells the whole story. Therefore, any patterns found require separate clinical trials to determine cause-and-effect.

# G. Epidemiology Concepts

### 1. What is the difference between prospective and retrospective studies?

Prospective studies follow participants over time, tracking them from the present day into the future to see how specific behaviors affect their health outcomes. Conversely, retrospective studies look backward at historical data, reviewing existing medical records or past participant histories to find links to current health conditions.

### 2. What is the difference between case-control and longitudinal/cohort studies?

Case-control studies compare people who already have a specific condition ("cases") to healthy individuals who do not ("controls") to look for past differences in their exposures. Longitudinal or cohort studies track a single, large group of people over an extended period to monitor how exposure to various risk factors is associated with the development of health conditions over time.

### 3. Based on your first look at AI-READI, what kinds of data are collected?

The AI-READI study collects a diverse, multimodal dataset divided into several key categories:

- **Clinical & Physiological Data:** Retinal imaging (OCT), standard laboratory blood work, and physical health vitals.

- **Continuous Wearable Sensor Data:** Continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and wearable activity trackers to monitor physical activity, sleep, and heart rate variability.

- **Environmental & Social Data:** Surveys tracking participant demographics, health literacy, and neighborhood social determinants of health (SDOH).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
